item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see how these guys are anomalous, but I don't buy the line that they are considered anomalous because they exhibit a behavior that is contrary to evolutionary advantage. Self destructive behavior is common.

The article just reads like it was written in a rush to get a draft out. The tone is that problematic.

And what the object does is squick for it's sake. It doesn't relate to a deeper fear about pregnancy or something like that. It's just… They want joeys, so they built themselves one.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Don’t mean to revive a dead discussion.

The term “foetus” should be replaced with “fetus” because that is the term recognized by the scientific community as correct and as such I feel the SCP foundation would keep with current scientific community terminology.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Well now. That is a weird article. It seems to me that with how vicious these little monsters are, it may behoove the Foundation to develop a version of SCP-027 that can be used to ease containment procedures. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Currently the only known population of SCP-1515 is being kept in Nature Reserve 12, an extended territory of Site 32, located 39 kilometers(24 miles) east of Alice Springs, Northern Territory, Australia. Any instances of SCP-1515 discovered outside of NR 12's perimeter are to be exterminated.

To this:

Currently the only known population of SCP-1515 is being kept in Nature Reserve 12, an extended territory of Site 32, located 39 kilometers(24 miles) east of Alice Springs, Northern Territory, Australia. Any instances of SCP-1515 discovered outside of NR 12's perimeter are to be exterminated. A version of SCP-027 is currently under development to assist in containment of captive instances.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, the containment protocols are so bare-bones it's immersion breaking. Sure, lock it up in a nature preserve… and then give the unredacted, exact location of said preserve and the site it's attached to! Further, let's not bother noting that Nature Preserve 12, and interaction with 1515 in general, should be off-limits to any personnel who are or may become pregnant? And, on top of that, there's not going to be any proactive management of a dangerous ("anomalous") animal that's near to a population center?

Sure, not every skip needs a Fort Knox, but common sense applies here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoting pretty much for everything stated above me, in much more succinct words

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Not all of them must be Montauks or unkillable reptiles" is a pretty mundane sentiment, and has been for years. In fact, I (mostly) double-checked this very discussion page, and I didn't see anybody downvoting because the anomaly wasn't big or scary enough (perhaps not quite anomalous enough, but that's different).

You should really get more engaged in the community before making assertions regarding its motivations and tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Deliciously creepy. People that downvoted ask too much of scips. Not all of them must be Montauks or unkillable reptiles. These small, nice, weird scips are what really keeps me coming to this site day and again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was sensing a Monty Python reference coming and was ready to downvote, but then it just veered right off into "WHAT THE HIGH HOLY F***?!"

I gave it a begrudging +1. I know it's essentially just a vicious little article with unnecessary gore, but hell, it got a reaction out of me I wasn't expecting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like this. The sewing bit killed it for me. No mention of how it's not normal for a rodent to use tools for one thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost wanted to upvote, but ultimately what Litfried says is very much how I feel about it. The idea is grisly and I love it, but something about it just doesn't seem "anomalous" enough —- I could easily see this as being a news story or urban legend stemming from a gross misinterpretation of some unrelated events in which these animals killed and mutilated random women, one of which might've been pregnant. That they find the creatures toying with the rotting fetuses doesn't seem like enough of a jump from "abnormal animal psychology" to "SECURE THE CONTAIN AND PROTECT"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels like buckets of squick just for squick's sake. Once you get beyond that, I don't think it's interesting enough. Species that engages in detrimental behaviour and keeps doing it just isn't that exciting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wallaby Wannabes sounds like a band name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so this little furry serial killer does gruesome shit that is actually bad for the species and it shouldn't even have survived.

The problem I have with this — besides the random gore — is not that "this is why they're anomalous" is a weak excuse to these rodents surviving more than two generations while endangering themselves by carrying out blood rituals. It's that the Foundation is incredibly casual about it. "This shit makes no sense. Huh. Who woulda know, right?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it is a species that survives in the wild while performing an activity that is only detrimental to them - wait, actually pretty consistently kills them - and serves no purpose whatsoever.

No. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who has the Arrogant Worm's song "Wallaby Wannabe" running through their heads while reading this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy. SCPs like this always make me regret living near the woods, and always make me happy to be a man. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit, why'd you have to make me remember this one?
I do think the concept is gripping enough to go without a narrative. And I really like that last line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always loved this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to come here to say how the last line killed the tone, but then I realized.
It's an interesting idea, with decent writing. Upvoted.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is up for review. Check it out!

I actually don't have much to say on this one. It's some basic body horror, executed in a pretty good way. I have tried to think of other stuff to say about this, but I just can't think of anything. I just like the decent body horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry corbette.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, after coming back to this more than a year later, I've decided to make a small change in order to address a lot of the concerns about the article. I've added a short disclaimer into the second footnote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, but foetus is actually an alternate spelling of fetus, and in my opinion makes it seem a bit creepier. I know it's not technically the medically acceptable spelling, but fuck it, I like how it looks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed a problem where "foetus" was supposed to be "fetus"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, by all rights this species should never have evolved, or at least should have died out quite rapidly if it somehow did come into existence. And yet, it hasn't. That's why it's an SCP and not just a weird, gory species - nature already has plenty of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed one small grammar issue. I hope that's OK…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ever heard the phrase "There's method to his/her/it's/my madness"?
There isn't here.
It's just weirdness for weirdness' sake. I don't see a "hook" or intrigue, it's just a "Well okay….that happened"

On a science fiction point of view, it's unrealistic.
Somebody mentioned a little while ago that "bats" would trigger it, and you claimed there are no bats in the outback.
Well, unfortunately, there are.
http://landforwildlifealicesprings.blogspot.com.au/2012/02/central-australian-bats-with-dennis.html
and I quote "They're certainly about and are actually quite common."
So here we have a species that displays compulsive self detrimental behaviour, to the point of being compelled to chase down flying mamals, invariably leading to exhaustion since your text doesn't have any mention of their seeming bloodlust every expiring.
Unless by encountering you mean a physical very close proximity encounter.
But even then, Look at any place in the history of every living thing on earth; If it does something detrimental to the continued existence of it's species, it's species discontinues.

If you could explain how, somehow, it manages to remain a living species, or at least show evidence that tests and examinations are being done, I could think there's reason here.
but it just seems….silly that the foundation would go "Here's something that shouldn't be….whatever"

I'm not saying "Give us the answers"
Just tell us "There might be an answer" or "The foundation is looking for an answer"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one noticing a growing number of lolAustralia articles?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to like this, but ultimately I don't.

Yes, I can understand why the Foundation cares enough to contain this. But I'm just not sure I buy it. How are these things surviving, and why isn't the Foundation investigating that? Eh. And the over-the-top-ness of the 'zomg they do this while the mother is STILL alive!!!' feels like a contrivance to tack on more horror.

Great choice of creature, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Among other issues, I kinda have the same problem with this as other people did with the first draft of Mr. Sillybug: it's basically an animal that behaves in an unusual- but not really anomalous- manner. Is a piranha an SCP? A honey badger? A lemming? A hydrothermal vent worm?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What in the fucking hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D'awww so adorable

OH GOD KILL IT WITH FIRE!
thats horrible, especially how it makes the pouch and all of that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of people seem to be liking this, but I just can't. I'm not sure if this is an adaptation of some off Scottish legend or what, but this is just too, well, not good. There seems to be no real coherence or main idea behind this creature. Why does it do this? There's no reason, and it can die from this behavior. Why does it create a pouch out of the "still living" mother's body? Why does it do anything other than to be gruesome and offensive?

Honestly, this thing is the next step up from a guro story, and if that's what gets people going on this website, I'd be happy to start writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adorahorrible. And this is, strangely enough, the second time in a week that I've had cause to 'coin' that word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That mental image is both adorable and horrible. Adorrible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Study? Yes. Special containment procedures? I don't see the need. Send some grad students down there with a National Geographic photographer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah. The failure of this is its strength in this realm - the fact that such a behaviour propagates over generations points to something being very off, and needs study

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote. These things would be worth studying due to their ABnormal behavior, but there is no real need for containment due to their lack of PARAnormal activity. There's other creatures in the world that do things about as weird as this. Public knowledge of these would not need to be suppressed. But the idea and the writing is too good for me to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, if I could only +1 this…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"So we had this idea; see we have this one SCP where if anything happens to the girl, or to the cosmic-horror-fetus inside the girl, it's the end of everything as we know it. So we stuck it in a room with something that would kill the girl, chew out her fetus, and stitch up a pouch from the girl's skin to stick the fetus in, which would kill the fetus and probably the rodent.

About 20 seconds in, the entirety of existence turned inside out and everyone died."

The end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whaaaat theeeee heeelllll!?
….
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the fuck did I just read? Why do I want to click the +1 button?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah. These guys are mostly mundane. That would have about the same result as if we tried a C-section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I'm wondering if these guys could be used to cure 231 by [DATA EXPUNGING] her hellfetus thing. Send a cenobite-bunny to kill a demonic [DATA EXPUNGED].

Sounds like a Tale idea to me…..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm absolutely floored with the in depth analysis of this article.

I… I don't even. I'll consult Sal for a "rewrite" within the week but I'm not sure if it will be possible due to my sudden lack of motor conas;f,l;ldvsdlv.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with Smapti on this. There's an all right idea here, but it isn't taken in the right direction. These things would just kill themselves off, between the infections and their attempted attacks on much larger animals. It doesn't really matter that they have larger teeth. It takes a lot to get through vertebrae, and something this size isn't going to have the jaw strength to take down anything much larger than it is. Their agility doesn't matter if they can't actually take down their prey.

These are kind of reminding me of the Surgeon Crabs, now that I think about it. Swarm something, immobilize it. Except the Surgeon Crabs aren't derping themselves to extinction and have a more plausible mode of attack.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did either of the people who wrote this abomination up watch Disaster Movie shortly before they came up with this idea? Because all I can think of is the scene where the rabid chipmunks maul a pregnant woman to death and chew on her spine.

I'm not downvoting because you reminded me of a terrible film, but it certainly didn't help. My main problem with this is how little sense it makes to me (for the reasons that Anaxa mentioned above).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tool use in a rodent and apparently believing themselves to be a different class of animal altogether strike me as 'unusual intelligence.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon reading this again, i'm withdrawing my upvote, but not for the reasons mentioned above.

It's interesting, for sure, and gruesome, but it just feels like there's not really any reason for the Foundation to care about these things. Their behavior, while unusual, isn't paranormal and doesn't show any particular signs of unusual intelligence, and they're not any more threatening to humans than any of several dozen other species found in the Australian outback. Public knowledge of their existence might prompt some comments of "Huh, that's weird", and "Wow, Australia is hardcore", but it would hardly constitute a threat to normalcy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: don't you mean… Wallabees?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guys, guise, srsly, how else are they going to be Wallabes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon encountering another placental mammal in the first trimester of pregnancy, SCP-1515 will begin to viciously attack the subject, using it large teeth to sever the spine through the back of the neck, causing paralysis in the victim.

Tim: That's the most foul, cruel, and bad-tempered rodent you ever set eyes on!
Sir Robin: You tit! I soiled my armor I was so scared!
Tim: Look, that rabbit's got a vicious streak a mile wide! It's a killer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Solid tone, and I see some interesting things behind the concept, but I have to downvote. The first thing that jumps out to me is that it is, frankly, absurdly lurid. Chewing the skin off the still-living mother? I can see why that's there thematically, but the line itself isn't delivered clinically, and, well, it's just too Rob Zombie to work on its own merits. More than that, though, we've already got quite a few things on this wiki that address pregnancy anxieties, among other issues, and while this takes that in a somewhat different direction, it seems to do so with too much…relish, I suppose? The article feels like it's trying to horrify me more than it's trying to serve it's own concept. It's good craftsmanship but I think the design is flawed. Sorry, Sal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is brutal, and that is something that is hard to extract out of a cute little critter. All props to you. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thought pattern was… in herbivores the only way this'd have had any chance of evolving would be shortage of appropriate nutrition.

As an aside , that lampshade certainly made it better. The foundation does contain anomalies, but they definitely won't pass over something this insane without going WTFF at it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those are both good suggestions, actually. I'll talk to famine about them. I also added in a footnote, after further thought, to address your complaint. I hope it makes it at least make a bit more sense in your eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's perfectly understandable. I might not like this , but at the time of writing, there's over 22 people who do.

As an aside.. it'd probably be a cheap, fail rehash of the White Knight, but there was this image crossing my mind of a researcher assigned to long-term experimentation with these who after watching it day to day begins to feel certain urges.. starting with wearing a saran wrap band on his waist with some raw steak, and going worse…

As an aside.. the only thing to do with pregnancy occuring to me is getting rid of competitor's offspring.. perhaps this was formerly that sort of a mechanism ,which fucked up hard.. but then it's 4AM and my thoughts are going odd ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They would never encounter and elephant or a bat. The Australian outback is almost entirely populated by marsupials, so the biggest placental mammal they'd be encountering is the camel - not exactly a big threat to a pack of things that are as fast as squirrels. Not to mention their prey would be pregnant, which would probably slow it down considerably. I don't understand how not catching a bat would affect them. It's not like it says these things have a 100% success rate in catching and killing every pregnant placental mammal they come across.

The procedure is stated as taking 3 hours. Presumably that's enough time for sinew to dry off in the heat of the Australian outback. Who knows, maybe they even squeegee the blood off the sinew with they little springhare paws.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reverting this page back to how famine and I had posted it initially. I appreciate everybody's criticisms, but it's reaching this point where if I make one change, one person doesn't like it, so I change it around and another person doesn't. It's great that you all have something you'd like to see in this, but I've concluded that it's most striking in its original form. If somebody would like to see a change made, I'm going to have to ask for a suggestion on what to change as opposed to simply what is wrong. I don't want to seem like I'm having a temper tantrum or anything here, but at this point there's not much I can do without changing the entire article around and possibly destroying the original intent of it - something I wouldn't want to do.
